analogy: Think of "Resources and Development" through the chapter's own opening images: Everything available in our environment which can be used to satisfy our needs, provided, it is technologically accessible, economically feasible and culturally acceptable can be termed as ‘Resource’. The process of transformation of things available in our environment involves an interactive relationship between nature, technology... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 2 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II, DEVELOPMENT OF RESOURCES, RESOURCE PLANNING, 3 RESOURCES AND DEVELOPMENT. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Resources and Development is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that everything available in our environment which can be used to satisfy our needs, provided, it is technologically accessible, economically feasible and culturally acceptable can be termed as ‘Resource’.

The text explains that the process of transformation of things available in our environment involves an interactive relationship between nature, technology and institutions.

The text explains that human beings interact with nature through technology and create institutions to accelerate their economic development.

The text explains that do you think that resources are free gifts of nature as is assumed by many?

The text explains that resources are a function of human activities.

The text explains that human beings themselves are essential components of resources.

The text explains that they t r a n s f o r m m a t e r i a l a v a i l a b l e i n o u r environment into resources and use them.

The text explains that these resources can be classified in the following ways – (a) On the basis of origin – biotic and abiotic (b) On the basis of exhaustibility – renewable and non-renewable (c) On the basis of ownership – individual, community, national and international (d) On the basis of status of development – potential, developed stock and reserves.

The text explains that can you identify and name the various items used in making life comfortable in our villages and towns.

The text explains that list the items and name the material used in their making.

The text explains that 1.1: Interdependent relationship between nature, technology and institutions Fig.

The text explains that 2 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II DEVELOPMENT OF RESOURCES Resources are vital for human survival as well as for maintaining the quality of life.

The text explains that it was believed that resources are free gifts of nature.

The text explains that as a result, human beings used them indiscriminately and this has led to the following major problems. • Depletion of resources for satisfying the greed of a few individuals. • Accumulation of resources in few hands, which, in turn, divided the society into two segments i.e. haves and have nots or rich and poor. • Indiscriminate exploitation of resources has led to global ecological crises such as, global warming, ozone layer depletion, environmental pollution and land degradation.

The text explains that an equitable distribution of resources has become essential for a sustained quality of life and global peace.

The text explains that if the present trend of resource depletion by a few individuals and countries continues, the future of our planet is in danger.

The text explains that therefore, resource planning is essential for sustainable existence of all forms of life.

The text explains that sustainable development Sustainable economic development means ‘development should take place without damaging the environment, and development in the present should not compromise with the needs of the future generations.’ Rio de Janeiro Earth Summit, 1992 In June 1992, more than 100 heads of states met in Rio de Janeiro in Brazil, for the first International Earth Summit.

The text explains that one major objective of the Agenda 21 is that every local government should draw its own local Agenda 21.

The text explains that rESOURCE PLANNING Planning is the widely accepted strategy for judicious use of resources.

The text explains that it has importance in a country like India, which has enormous diversity in the availability of resources.

The text explains that there are regions which are rich in certain types of resources but are deficient in some other 1.

The text explains that ask questions about : (a) What do they think about resources they use?

The text explains that there are some regions which can be considered self sufficient in terms of the availability of resources and there are some regions which have acute shortage of some vital resources.

The text explains that for example, the states of Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh are rich in minerals and coal deposits.

The text explains that arunachal Pradesh has abundance of water resources but lacks in infrastructural development.

The text explains that the state of Rajasthan is very well endowed with solar and wind energy but lacks in water resources.

The text explains that it has very rich cultural heritage but it is deficient in water, infrastructure and some vital minerals.

The text explains that this calls for balanced resource planning at the national, state, regional and local levels. technology and institutions may hinder development.

The text explains that there are many regions in our country that are rich in resources but these are included in economically backward regions.

The text explains that on the contrary there are some regions which have a poor resource base but they are economically developed.

The text explains that can you name some resource rich but economically backward regions and some resource poor but economically developed regions?

The text explains that the history of colonisation reveals that rich resources in colonies were the main attractions for the foreign invaders.

The text explains that it was primarily the higher level of technological development of the colonising countries that helped them to exploit resources of other regions and establish their supremacy over the colonies.

The text explains that therefore, resources can contribute to development only when they are accompanied by appropriate technological development and institutional changes.

The text explains that therefore, in India, development, in general, and resource development in particular does not only involve the availability of resources, but also the technology, quality of human resources and the historical experiences of the people.

The text explains that conservation of Resources: Resources are vital for any developmental activity.

The text explains that but irrational consumption and over-utilisation of resources may lead to socio-economic and environmental problems.

The text explains that to overcome these problems, resource conservation at various levels is important.

The text explains that for example, Gandhiji was very apt in voicing his concern about resource conservation in these words: “There is enough Resource Planning in India Resource planning is a complex process which involves : (i) identification and inventory of resources across the regions of the country.

The text explains that imagine if oil supplies get exhausted, how will this affect our life style?

The text explains that solve the puzzle by following your search horizontally and vertically to find the hidden answers.

The text explains that (i) Natural endowments in the form of land, water, vegetation and minerals.

The text explains that (iii) Soil with high water retaining capacity.

The text explains that (iv) Intensively leached soils of the monsoon climate.

The text explains that 12 CONTEMPORARY INDIA – II S F G S F O B R O M S U A P J Q G A F F O R E S T A T I O N P N R E C P R S L D M I L N F S N A T Q X U O V A I O L A L O D E I D R J U J L D B N B D T G H M I N E R A L S A X M W B V J K M E D C R U P F M H R L A T E R I T E M V A Z T V L A B Z O E N M F T I S D L R C C G N N S Z I O P A X T Y J H K J G K D T D C S L S E G E W (i) Natural endowments in the form of land, water, vegetation and minerals.

The text explains that (v) Plantation of trees on a large scale to check soil erosion.

The text explains that (vi) The Great Plains of India are made up of these soils.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Contemporary India II, Chapter 1: Resources and Development
